text:
TheParis 2024 Olympicswill see 10,000 of the finest athletes in the world battling it out over 32 sports across 19 action-packed days.

At the end of each event, thegold, silver and bronze medallistsare presented with their prizes, posing for pictures on the podium in what is one of the proudest moments in their lives.

But what might seem a little odd is the customary pose involving athletes biting their medals.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for latest news on the Paris Olympics

The practice has become common in everyone from Olympians toPremier Leaguefootballers. But why exactly do athletes bite their medals?

The reason for athletes gnawing into their gold, silver and bronze medals has changed over time.

Traditionally, because gold is softer and more malleable than other metals, one way to determine whether or not it was real gold was to bite into it — if it’s authentic, your teeth would have left an indentation.

Today, most medal winners aren’t going to be too hung up on if it’s actual gold or not as it’s what the medal symbolises that matters.

For what it’s worth, Olympic gold medals aren’t full gold. They are required to be made from at least 92.5% silver, and must contain a minimum of six grams of gold.

So why do they still chow down into their medal?

It’s all about the optics, of course.

‘It’s become an obsession with the photographers,’ David Wallechinsky, president of the International Society of Olympic Historians, toldCNN.

‘I think they look at it as an iconic shot, as something that you can probably sell. I don’t think it’s something the athletes would probably do on their own.’

title: Why do Olympians bite their medals? Ritual explained | Metro News
description: Athletes, from footballers to Olympic gymnasts, will be photographed biting their medals after a competition at the Paris 2024 games.
